verses.text: Korah a Levite, Dathan and Abiram Reubenites, and 250 princes rose against Moses and Aaron: ye take too much upon you, all the congregation are holy — wherefore lift ye up yourselves above the LORD's assembly? Guidance: A theologically true sentence weaponized. Yes, every Israelite is holy — but holy in different callings. The rebellion borrows God's vocabulary to attack God's order. Flatter the crowd, drape it in scripture, and call it justice.

Moses fell on his face. Tomorrow the LORD will show who is His and who is holy. Take censers, Korah and all his company; put fire and incense before the LORD — the man whom the LORD chooses shall be holy. Ye take too much upon you, ye sons of Levi. Guidance: Moses doesn't argue, he kneels — then sets a test only God can decide. If the rebellion was right, fire would prove it. Spiritual authority is not seized; it's confirmed by the One who calls.

Moses to Korah: ye sons of Levi — seemeth it small that God hath separated you to His tabernacle, brought you near Himself? And ye seek the priesthood also? Against the LORD ye gather — what is Aaron that ye murmur against him? Guidance: Korah is already a Levite, God's hand on him. But the calling he had wasn't enough; he wanted Aaron's. Discontent with a real gift is the seedbed of rebellion. The murmur is against God, not the man.

Moses sent for Dathan and Abiram. They said: we will not come up. Is it a small thing thou hast brought us out of a land flowing with milk and honey to kill us in the wilderness? Wilt thou put out the eyes of these men? We will not come up. Guidance: They call Egypt — the land of bricks and tears — the land of milk and honey. Memory rewritten to fuel resentment. They refuse to even come and talk. Defiance closes the door before any conversation can open it.

Moses very wroth. Korah gathered the congregation against them at the tabernacle. The glory of the LORD appeared. The LORD: separate yourselves, I will consume them. Moses and Aaron fell on their faces: O God of the spirits of all flesh, shall one man sin and Thou be wroth with all the congregation? Guidance: Moses' first instinct under God's anger is intercession. He just survived insult, yet pleads for the insulters. God of the spirits of all flesh — He sees each individually, and Moses asks Him to.

The LORD: get up from about the tabernacle of Korah, Dathan, Abiram. Moses went; the elders followed. Depart from the tents of these wicked men, touch nothing of theirs. They departed. Dathan and Abiram stood at the door with wives, sons, little children. Guidance: God gives time to separate before judgment. The neighbors had to choose: stay near the rebellion or step away. The wives and little children at the door is the saddest line — they did not vote, but they bore the verdict.

Moses: hereby ye shall know the LORD hath sent me — if these men die the common death, the LORD hath not sent me. But if the LORD make a new thing — the earth open her mouth and swallow them, alive into the pit — then ye shall understand these men have provoked the LORD. Guidance: Moses stakes his whole ministry on the next thirty seconds. If they die of old age, he was a liar. He asks God to settle the question publicly, and lets God choose the manner.

As he finished, the ground clave asunder, the earth opened her mouth and swallowed them, their houses, all the men of Korah, and all their goods. They went down alive into the pit; the earth closed upon them. Israel fled at the cry. Fire from the LORD consumed the 250 men who offered incense. Guidance: The earth opens mid-sentence. No interval between Moses' last word and God's verdict. Then fire — a second sign, leaving no doubt. Rebellion against the called costs more than the rebel guessed.

The LORD to Eleazar: take the censers out of the burning — they are hallowed. Make them broad plates for a covering of the altar — a sign that no stranger that is not of the seed of Aaron come near to offer incense before the LORD. Guidance: The objects of rebellion become a sermon hammered into the altar. Every priest after will see the bronze of Korah's pride and remember: not everyone who lifts a censer is sent. The warning is permanent.

On the morrow all the congregation murmured against Moses and Aaron: ye have killed the people of the LORD. The cloud covered the tabernacle, the glory appeared. The LORD: get from this congregation, I will consume them. Moses and Aaron fell on their faces. Guidance: Twenty-four hours after the ground swallowed the rebels, the survivors blame the messenger. The human heart can witness a miracle and miss its meaning by morning. Moses falls on his face — again. Some leaders kneel more than they stand.

Moses to Aaron: take censer, fire from the altar, put on incense — wrath is gone out, the plague begun. Aaron ran into the congregation; he put on incense, made atonement, stood between the dead and the living — the plague was stayed. Died: 14,700, besides those about Korah. Guidance: The priest runs INTO the plague. The censer that killed rebels saves the people in the right hands. Aaron stands in the gap — a foreshadow of the One who would stand between us and death forever.
